title: Projects
text:
Cartographic modelling of old growth forests on Vancouver Island
        This project analyses the distribution of old growth forests across Vancouver Island, British Columbia, using forest inventory data and the BC Cumulative Effects Framework (CEF). The analysis identifies crown forest land, classifies stands by seral stage, and compares calculated old growth percentages against provincial targets by Landscape Unit and BEC subzone. Results are visualized as a cartographic map showing where forests fall short of, meet, or exceed old growth targets.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        Version Control and Feature Digitization for Geospatial Data
        This project explores version control for geospatial data using Git and Kart, and practices feature digitization in both QGIS and ArcGIS Pro. Campus tree locations at UBC Vancouver were edited and validated against a recent orthophoto using Kart's branching and merging capabilities. Five campus buildings were then manually digitized in ArcGIS Pro and compared against the official UBC building dataset to evaluate positional and geometric accuracy.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        Geocoding and Spatial Data Creation in Vancouver
        This project focuses on converting non-spatial data into geospatial datasets using ArcGIS Pro. Street tree addresses from a Vancouver neighbourhood were geocoded using a custom address locator built from a regional road network. Park tree coordinates were converted from tabular data into a spatial layer, and tree density was calculated per park across the City of Vancouver. Results were visualized on a final map showing geocoded trees, park tree density, and road infrastructure.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        Raster Analysis and Least Cost Path Modelling for Grizzly Bear Habitat
        This project models Grizzly Bear movement across the Yellowhead Bear Management Area (BMA) in the Rocky Mountain foothills of western Alberta, Canada. Using raster analysis tools in GDAL, QGIS, and ArcGIS Pro, a cost surface was built from terrain, land cover, and road proximity data. A least cost path was then derived to identify the most feasible movement corridor between core and secondary Grizzly Bear habitat.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        Terrain Analysis and Riparian Area Management in the Nahmint Valley
        This project defines riparian reserve and management zones within a topographically complex forested watershed in the Nahmint Valley, British Columbia, using a high-resolution Digital Elevation Model (DEM). Stream networks were derived and characterized by order, gradient, and width. BC government stream classification guidelines were applied to assign buffer distances for Riparian Management Areas (RMAs), and watersheds were delineated to assess how forest harvesting runoff could impact stream networks.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        Salmon Stream Network Analysis on Vancouver Island
        This project models Pacific salmon spawning habitat across Vancouver Island, British Columbia, using hydrological analysis and network tracing in ArcGIS Pro. A stream network was derived from a Digital Elevation Model (DEM), dams were modelled as barriers, and linear referencing was used to characterize stream segments by slope gradient and stream order. The network was then traced from the ocean to identify which stream segments are accessible to salmon and which are blocked by dams, with results filtered against basic habitat requirements.
      
    
  

  
    
      
        Spatial Interpolation and Visualization of LiDAR Data
        This project uses LiDAR data collected over the UBC Malcolm Knapp Research Forest (MKRF) in Maple Ridge, British Columbia, to generate and evaluate Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) using multiple spatial interpolation approaches. Ground return points were extracted, filtered, and processed using PDAL, and three interpolation methods — Natural Neighbor, Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW), and Spline — were compared against a binned reference DEM using zonal statistics across elevation and slope classes.

title: Agness Chisale
text:
About Me
I am a geospatial analyst and environmental scientist passionate about using spatial data, GIS and remote sensing to support environmental management and data-driven decision-making. I am currently pursuing a Master’s degree in Geomatics for Environmental Management at the University of British Columbia. I also hold a Bachelor’s degree in Environmental Science and Technology from the University of Malawi, earned with distinction. I am technically fluent in ArcGIS Pro, QGIS, PostgreSQL/PostGIS, Python and R.

title: Experiences
text:
Geospatial Analyst (Volunteer)

Tayo Climate Partners • 2025 - Present • Remote
Voluntary geospatial role supporting blue carbon and coastal restoration projects. Work covers data pre-processing, spatial analysis workflows, and site suitability assessments to evaluate the potential of sites for mangrove and other ecosystem restoration initiatives.
Tools: GIS, ArcGIS, Python, R



Environmental Officer

Lilongwe City Council • July 2024 - May 2025 • Lilongwe, Malawi
Hands-on environmental planning role focused on urban development compliance and impact assessment. Work covered spatial data analysis, environmental auditing, stakeholder engagement, and regulatory monitoring across infrastructure and waste projects.
Tools: GIS, ArcGIS, ESMPs, Environmental Auditing, Stakeholder Consultation



Environmental Consultant

Water, Waste and Environmental Consultants • August 2022 - June 2024 • Lilongwe, Malawi
Consulting role focused on environmental and social impact assessment across energy and infrastructure projects. Work covered field data collection, geospatial analysis, and statistical modelling to inform compliance decisions and mitigation strategies.
Tools: GIS, ArcGIS, SPSS, STATA
